old_claim.note: Digital Photography (CCD/CMOS Sensors) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The first video endoscopes depended on miniaturized CCD image sensors that converted the distal optical image into an electronic video signal.

source_shape.source_locator: Review history section discussing miniaturized charge-coupled devices and the first video endoscope.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The review states that miniaturization of a charged-coupled device allowed Welch Allyn to release the first video endoscope in 1983.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a required CCD-sensor edge because the electronic sensor is the mechanism that makes the endoscope a video endoscope rather than a purely optical fiberscope.

ontology_before: Video endoscopy treated CCD/CMOS image sensors as a generic enabler.
ontology_after: Video endoscopy now treats electronic image sensors as the required video-capture component.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the CCD/CMOS sensor edge is required.
would_reject_if: The node were re-scoped to all flexible fiberoptic endoscopy rather than video endoscopy systems.
A source showed the first video endoscopes did not use electronic image sensors.
The graph removed the fiber-optic predecessor context while strengthening the sensor edge.
short_reason: Video endoscopy requires an electronic image sensor, not merely generic photography context.
